heroOverview: Kuru is a fatal prion disease of the brain caused by the misfolding and aggregation of normal human prion protein (PrP), formerly transmitted through the ritual consumption of human neural tissue among the Fore people of Papua New Guinea. Between the 1950s and 1960s, kuru killed an estimated 1,100–2,000 Fore people, making it the most lethal epidemic of a human prion disease ever documented in a defined population. Transmission ceased once mortuary cannibalism was abandoned after 1960, yet cases continued to appear for decades because the incubation period can exceed 50 years — the longest confirmed incubation in any human prion disorder. Today kuru has largely disappeared, but it remains medically important as the disease that first established prions as infectious agents and shaped our understanding of variant Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease, bovine spongiform encephalopathy, and the wider family of transmissible spongiform encephalopathies.

keyStats.prevalence: Effectively eliminated; peak incidence was roughly 200 deaths/year among ~8,000 Fore people in the late 1950s
keyStats.demographics: Historically affected Fore women and children far more than men (women and children participated in neural-tissue consumption more often)
keyStats.avgAge: Onset ranged from childhood to late adulthood; incubation period 5–50+ years
keyStats.globalCases: ~2,700 total deaths recorded before kuru was eliminated; no confirmed new cases since the early 2000s

definition: Kuru (ICD-10: A81.81) is a transmissible spongiform encephalopathy — a prion disease — caused by the conformational conversion of normal cellular prion protein (PrP-C) to an abnormal, protease-resistant isoform (PrP-Sc). The misfolded protein accumulates in neurons, triggering vacuolation of grey matter, neuronal loss, reactive astrogliosis, and the formation of amyloid plaques, giving brain tissue a sponge-like appearance on autopsy. The word 'kuru' means 'trembling with fear' in the Fore language of the Eastern Highlands of Papua New Guinea. It was transmitted by endocannibalistic mortuary practices in which mourners — predominantly women and children — handled and consumed the brains and organs of deceased relatives. Kuru falls within the broader category of human prion diseases, which also includes sporadic Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease (sCJD), variant CJD (vCJD), fatal familial insomnia, and Gerstmann-Sträussler-Scheinker syndrome. Unlike most infectious diseases, no virus, bacterium, or classical nucleic acid-based pathogen is involved; the infectious agent is the misfolded protein itself. Kuru progresses through three clinical stages — ambulatory, sedentary, and terminal — and is uniformly fatal, typically within 3 to 24 months of symptom onset.

typesClassification.description: The single recognised form, characterised by cerebellar ataxia followed by dementia, occurring after endocannibalistic exposure to neural tissue of an infected individual.
Cases arising within 5–12 years of exposure, generally observed in individuals who participated in cannibalism in the late 1950s and died before 1970.
Cases with incubation periods exceeding 30 years; the longest confirmed case had an incubation of approximately 56 years, in a person exposed before 1960 who died in 2009.

primarySymptoms: Progressive cerebellar ataxia — unsteady, wide-based gait that worsens over weeks to months and is the hallmark of the ambulatory stage.
Postural tremor affecting the trunk and limbs, typically noticed by the patient and family early in the illness and becoming disabling over time.
Dysarthria — slurring or slowing of speech caused by cerebellar involvement of the muscles used for articulation.
Titubation — rhythmic oscillation of the head or trunk during sitting, characteristic of midline cerebellar dysfunction.
Emotional lability — inappropriate laughter, crying, or mood swings, which gave kuru the local name 'laughing sickness'.
Convergent strabismus and other ocular motor abnormalities, reflecting brainstem involvement as disease advances.
Profound muscle weakness and wasting in the sedentary stage, leaving patients unable to walk or stand without assistance.
Loss of deep tendon reflexes (areflexia) in many patients, distinguishing kuru from some other prion diseases.
Dementia and cognitive deterioration, appearing relatively late in the sedentary stage and progressing through the terminal phase.
Dysphagia — difficulty swallowing — developing in the terminal stage and often leading to aspiration pneumonia.
Urinary and faecal incontinence occurring in the terminal stage as cortical and subcortical involvement becomes severe.
Decubitus ulcers and cachexia in the final weeks as patients become completely immobile.
earlyWarningSigns: Subtle unsteadiness when walking, especially on uneven ground.
A fine, barely perceptible tremor in the hands noticed during precise tasks.
Mild slurring of speech or slower articulation.
Difficulty maintaining balance during tandem gait (walking heel-to-toe).
emergencySigns: Inability to swallow, raising the risk of aspiration pneumonia.
Complete loss of the ability to walk or sit upright without support.
Sudden worsening of consciousness or new-onset seizures.
Fever with respiratory deterioration, suggesting aspiration pneumonia requiring urgent care.

causes.description: Transmission occurred through Fore mortuary practices in which women and children handled and consumed the brain and spinal cord of deceased relatives. The oral, conjunctival, and cutaneous routes are thought responsible; prion protein survives cooking temperatures and is concentrated in neural tissue.

causes.cause: Misfolded prion protein (PrP-Sc) as the infectious agent
causes.description: Ingested PrP-Sc acts as a template that induces conformational change in the host's own normal PrP-C, converting it to the pathological isoform. This self-propagating protein misfolding amplifies through the nervous system without any nucleic acid involvement.

The molecular pathway from initial prion seeding to sufficient neuronal loss to produce symptoms takes 5 to 56 years depending on dose, route, and host genetics. During this silent period, no test can detect the accumulating pathology in routine clinical settings.

causes.cause: Host genetic susceptibility at codon 129 of PRNP
causes.description: The PRNP gene codon 129 encodes either methionine (M) or valine (V). In kuru-exposed Fore populations, heterozygous individuals (MV) proved strongly protected against clinical disease, and many long-incubation survivors carry the MV genotype, suggesting this polymorphism modulates both susceptibility and incubation length.

Brain tissue carries the highest prion load; peripheral tissues carry far less. Higher infectious dose — more neural tissue consumed — is associated with shorter incubation periods, consistent with dose–response relationships seen in experimental prion transmission.

riskFactors.description: The sole transmission route historically identified; women and children bore the greatest exposure risk because mortuary customs assigned them the role of handling and consuming neural tissue.

MM individuals in the kuru-exposed Fore population developed disease more readily and at shorter incubation times. The protective effect of MV heterozygosity is one of the clearest documented examples of natural human selection by an infectious prion disease.

Children exposed in the 1950s had a broader window of susceptibility, and some developed disease within a few years; others with the same exposure lived into old age before succumbing, consistent with genetic modulation.

riskFactors.factor: Female sex during the epidemic era
riskFactors.description: Fore custom restricted brain and organ consumption primarily to women and children, producing a female-to-male mortality ratio of approximately 8:1 at the peak of the epidemic.

A unique PRNP variant (G127V) found at elevated frequency among Fore survivors appears to confer near-complete resistance to kuru and possibly to other prion diseases, likely arising through strong selective pressure during the epidemic.

affectedDemographics: Fore people of the Eastern Highlands Province, Papua New Guinea — the only population in which kuru was endemic
Women and children historically, due to mortuary practice customs
Adults of both sexes who participated in cannibalism before 1960
Long-incubation cases detected in elderly Fore adults through the 1980s–2000s
diagnosisOverview: Kuru is a clinical and epidemiological diagnosis. No blood test, CSF assay, or imaging finding is pathognomonic during life, and definitive confirmation requires neuropathological examination of brain tissue at autopsy. The diagnosis is suspected in any individual with a credible history of exposure to Fore mortuary practices (now historically remote) who presents with progressive cerebellar ataxia, tremor, and emotional lability. Routine blood and CSF tests are largely normal. CSF protein is typically only mildly elevated, and markers of neuronal injury such as 14-3-3 protein may be detectable in some cases but are neither sensitive nor specific for kuru. MRI brain may show cerebellar atrophy and, in some cases, white matter signal changes, but these are non-specific. EEG does not typically show the periodic sharp wave complexes seen in sporadic CJD. Autopsy reveals spongiform change, neuronal loss, astrogliosis, and kuru plaques — amyloid deposits composed of PrP-Sc, particularly prominent in the cerebellum, which distinguishes kuru pathologically from most cases of sporadic CJD. Genetic testing for PRNP codon 129 polymorphism is available and can provide supporting evidence, but has no diagnostic threshold on its own.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Documents the three-stage progression — ambulatory ataxia, sedentary tremor and dementia, terminal immobility — which forms the cornerstone of the clinical diagnosis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A neurologist performs a detailed assessment of gait, coordination, reflexes, eye movements, and cognition; the pattern of predominantly cerebellar signs without early dementia is characteristic.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses cerebellar atrophy, white matter changes, and rules out structural or vascular causes of ataxia.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You lie still in a scanner for approximately 30–45 minutes; the images show brain structure in detail but may appear near-normal early in kuru.

diagnosticTests.test: CSF analysis including 14-3-3 protein and RT-QuIC
diagnosticTests.purpose: CSF 14-3-3 and tau are markers of rapid neuronal damage; real-time quaking-induced conversion (RT-QuIC) detects misfolded prion protein with high sensitivity in other prion diseases and may be applied in suspected kuru.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A lumbar puncture (spinal tap) is performed under local anaesthesia; CSF is sent for cell count, protein, glucose, 14-3-3, tau, and RT-QuIC.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Can help exclude other encephalopathies; unlike sporadic CJD, kuru does not produce the classic 1–2 Hz periodic sharp wave complexes on EEG.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Electrodes are attached to the scalp for approximately 30–60 minutes while brain electrical activity is recorded; the test is painless.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies codon 129 genotype (MM/MV/VV) and any pathological mutations; informs prognosis and, in research settings, characterises resistance variants like G127V.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood sample is sent to a genetics laboratory; results take days to weeks depending on the service.

diagnosticTests.test: Brain autopsy with immunohistochemistry for PrP-Sc
diagnosticTests.purpose: Provides definitive confirmation of prion disease and distinguishes kuru (prominent kuru plaques in cerebellum) from other subtypes.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed post-mortem; brain tissue is stained with prion-specific antibodies and examined microscopically.

treatmentOverview: There is no curative or disease-modifying treatment for kuru. No antiviral, antibiotic, immunosuppressant, or experimental compound has been shown to halt or reverse prion-induced neuronal damage in a human clinical trial. All care is supportive and palliative, aimed at maintaining function as long as possible, preventing complications, and ensuring comfort in what is always a terminal illness. The disease is managed across three overlapping domains: neurological monitoring, physical rehabilitation, and palliative medicine. In the ambulatory stage, physiotherapy focuses on gait safety, balance training, and the early introduction of mobility aids such as walking frames or wheelchairs. Speech and language therapy provides swallowing assessments and texture guidance as dysarthria and dysphagia emerge. Nutritional support — oral supplements, then texture modification, and ultimately nasogastric or PEG feeding according to patient and family wishes — is central to minimising cachexia. Pain and distress are managed with appropriate analgesics and anxiolytics; seizures in the terminal phase are treated with standard antiepileptic agents for comfort. End-of-life planning including advance care directives and lasting power of attorney should be completed while the patient retains decisional capacity, typically early in the sedentary stage. Research efforts have historically included quinacrine and pentosan polysulphate trials in sporadic CJD, but neither demonstrated efficacy, and no specific agent has reached clinical trial stage for kuru given its effective elimination as a disease. Current prion research focuses on small molecules that stabilise the normal PrP-C conformation, antisense oligonucleotides that reduce PrP-C expression, and passive immunotherapy strategies, none yet proven in humans but representing the most promising directions for the broader prion disease family.

medicalTreatments.name: Palliative and supportive care
medicalTreatments.description: The core of management, encompassing symptom control (analgesia, antispasmodics, anxiolytics), pressure-area care, oral hygiene, and multidisciplinary team co-ordination. Initiated from diagnosis and intensified as the disease progresses through its three stages.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Supportive care reduces preventable complications such as aspiration pneumonia and pressure ulcers, improving comfort for 100% of patients even though disease progression is not altered.

medicalTreatments.name: Physiotherapy and occupational therapy
medicalTreatments.description: Targeted exercises and adaptive equipment in the ambulatory stage help maintain gait safety and independence longer; assistive devices (walker, wheelchair) are introduced as ataxia worsens.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Functional aids have been shown in other neurodegenerative diseases to extend independent mobility by weeks to months; no kuru-specific trial data exist.

medicalTreatments.name: Nutritional support and dysphagia management
medicalTreatments.description: Speech and language therapy assessment of swallowing helps guide texture modification of food and fluids; in late-stage disease, enteral feeding via nasogastric or PEG tube may be discussed in accordance with the patient's and family's wishes.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Dysphagia management reduces aspiration pneumonia risk by approximately 30–50% in comparable neurodegenerative conditions.

medicalTreatments.name: Antiepileptic drugs if seizures occur
medicalTreatments.description: A minority of prion disease patients develop seizures in the terminal phase; standard antiepileptic agents are used for symptom management rather than disease modification.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Seizure control is achieved in approximately 60–80% of cases with appropriate antiepileptics, improving comfort in the terminal stage.

alternativeTreatments.name: Spiritual and community support
alternativeTreatments.description: Among Fore communities, traditional healing practices and communal grief processes have cultural importance; these are supported within a palliative framework and do not conflict with medical management.

alternativeTreatments.name: Research trial participation
alternativeTreatments.description: In the era when kuru cases were still occurring, participation in longitudinal research studies (as at the NIH kuru project) provided access to specialist neurological assessment and contributed to understanding of prion biology; patients and families were informed of research aims.

whySeeSpecialist: Any patient suspected of having a prion disease should be assessed by a neurologist with prion disease expertise, ideally at a national prion disease surveillance centre. Specialist assessment confirms the clinical stage, arranges appropriate investigations including RT-QuIC and MRI, provides genetic counselling regarding PRNP findings for family members, and co-ordinates palliative and community care. Early specialist involvement also ensures cases are notified to national surveillance systems, which is important for epidemiological monitoring.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a neurologist with specific experience in prion diseases or movement disorders, affiliated with a national CJD surveillance centre (such as the UK National CJD Research and Surveillance Unit in Edinburgh or the NIH Prion Disease Clinic). Ask whether the centre submits cases to national and international surveillance registries. Palliative care physicians experienced in rapidly progressive neurological disease should be involved from an early stage.
preventionStrategies: Cessation of endocannibalistic practices — the single intervention that eliminated kuru from the Fore population after 1960.
Safe handling of neural tissue in surgical, laboratory, and mortuary settings using universal prion decontamination protocols (autoclaving at 134°C for 18 minutes or sodium hypochlorite treatment).
Reporting of any suspected prion disease to national surveillance authorities to enable early epidemiological response.
Pre-surgical testing and precautions in neurosurgery to prevent iatrogenic prion transmission, particularly for instruments contacting high-risk neural tissues.
No human growth hormone derived from cadaveric pituitary (replaced by recombinant product since 1985) to prevent iatrogenic CJD transmission.
lifestyleModifications: There are no lifestyle modifications that prevent or slow kuru progression once symptoms begin.
For carers: seek specialist palliative support early, learn safe positioning and transfer techniques to prevent carer injury and patient falls.
Maintain nutrition with texture-modified foods and thickened fluids as swallowing deteriorates.
Regular repositioning (every 2 hours) to prevent pressure ulcers once mobility is severely limited.

recommended: High-calorie, high-protein diet to counteract wasting in the ambulatory and sedentary stages
Texture-modified soft or pureed foods if swallowing becomes impaired
Thickened liquids if a speech therapist identifies silent aspiration on swallowing assessment
Oral nutritional supplements to maintain body weight when intake declines
avoid: Hard, dry, or crumbly foods once dysphagia develops, due to choking and aspiration risk
Thin liquids without thickening if swallow assessment identifies penetration or aspiration
Any food or beverage that the patient reports causes coughing or difficulty during meals

exerciseGuidelines: In the ambulatory stage, low-impact balance and strengthening exercises under physiotherapy supervision can help maintain gait safety and delay the need for a walking aid. As the sedentary stage is reached, active exercise is replaced by passive range-of-motion therapy and positioning to prevent contractures. In the terminal stage, gentle passive movements help maintain comfort and skin integrity.
dailyManagement: Arrange a falls risk assessment and install grab rails, non-slip flooring, and a hospital bed at home as early as the sedentary stage.
Schedule regular speech therapy reviews to monitor swallowing and update dietary texture recommendations.
Maintain a skin inspection routine and use pressure-relieving mattresses and cushions once mobility is reduced.
Ensure legal and advance care planning documents (lasting power of attorney, advance directive) are completed while the patient retains decision-making capacity.
Connect with national prion disease patient support and family networks for psychological and practical support.
prognosis: Kuru is uniformly fatal. Once clinical symptoms appear, the average survival from onset to death has been reported as approximately 12 months (range 3 months to 2 years in most historical case series, with occasional outliers surviving longer). Death is most commonly caused by aspiration pneumonia, sepsis from pressure ulcers, or cachexia. There is no documented case of spontaneous recovery or long-term survival after symptom onset. The discovery of protective PRNP variants (G127V) in kuru-exposed Fore survivors offers the important scientific insight that genetic resistance can be acquired through epidemic selection, informing future therapeutic strategies for all prion diseases. In the absence of exposure, the general population bears no meaningful risk of kuru.
recoveryTimeline: There is no recovery from kuru. The clinical course moves through three stages: the ambulatory stage (ataxia, tremor, months of relative independence) lasting a median of roughly 6 months; the sedentary stage (inability to walk without support, worsening tremor and dementia) lasting weeks to months; and the terminal stage (bed-bound, dysphagia, incontinence, cachexia) lasting days to weeks before death. Total survival from first symptoms is typically 12–24 months, though incubation periods before any symptoms span years to decades.
complications: Aspiration pneumonia — the leading direct cause of death, arising from progressive dysphagia and loss of airway protective reflexes in the terminal stage.
Malnutrition and severe cachexia — caused by dysphagia, immobility, and the metabolic demands of systemic illness.
Pressure ulcers — develop rapidly in immobile, malnourished patients and can progress to deep tissue infection and sepsis.
Urinary tract infections and urosepsis — secondary to urinary incontinence and immobility in the terminal stage.
Falls and fractures — a significant risk in the ambulatory and early sedentary stages because of ataxia and postural instability.
Respiratory failure — the final pathway in most terminal-stage patients, compounded by aspiration, immobility, and progressive neurological decline.

supportResources.name: UK National CJD Research and Surveillance Unit (NCJDRSU)
supportResources.description: The UK national centre for prion disease surveillance, clinical enquiries, and family support, providing information for clinicians and patients affected by prion diseases including kuru.

supportResources.name: NIH National Institute of Neurological Disorders and Stroke — Prion Diseases
supportResources.description: Patient-facing overview of prion diseases from the U.S. National Institute of Neurological Disorders and Stroke.

supportResources.name: CJD Support Network (UK)
supportResources.description: UK charity providing emotional and practical support to families affected by all forms of prion disease, including kuru.

sources.title: Collinge J et al. Kuru in the 21st century — an acquired human prion disease with very long incubation periods. Lancet, 2006
Mead S et al. Genetic prion disease caused by PRNP codon 196 mutation: natural history study. Brain, 2006 — PRNP G127V resistance variant in kuru
Prusiner SB. Prions. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, 1998 (Nobel lecture)
Gajdusek DC. Unconventional viruses and the origin and disappearance of kuru. Science, 1977 (Nobel lecture)
Mead S et al. A novel protective prion protein variant that colocalizes with kuru exposure. New England Journal of Medicine, 2009
Liberski PP et al. Kuru: genes, cannibalism and neuropathology. Neuropathology and Applied Neurobiology, 2012

confusedWithConditions.name: Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease (sporadic)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Sporadic CJD presents with rapid dementia and myoclonus with characteristic EEG changes; kuru begins with cerebellar ataxia and emotional lability, and lacks periodic sharp waves on EEG. CJD is not acquired through cannibalism.

confusedWithConditions.name: Variant Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: vCJD is acquired through eating BSE-contaminated beef, affects younger patients, and shows a characteristic posterior thalamic 'pulvinar' MRI sign; kuru was acquired through consumption of human tissue in Papua New Guinea.

confusedWithConditions.name: Multiple sclerosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: MS causes relapsing or progressive neurological deficits with characteristic MRI white matter lesions and CSF oligoclonal bands; kuru is uniformly progressive without remission and lacks demyelinating lesion patterns.

relatedConditions.name: Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease
relatedConditions.relevance: CJD is the most common human prion disease and shares the same fundamental pathogenesis — PrP-Sc accumulation — as kuru; the study of kuru directly led to recognition of CJD as a transmissible prion disorder.

relatedConditions.name: Variant Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease
relatedConditions.relevance: vCJD arises from dietary bovine prion exposure analogous to kuru's oral transmission route, and insights from kuru epidemiology informed the BSE-vCJD public health response.

relatedConditions.name: Multiple sclerosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Both are serious, progressive neurological conditions that cause cerebellar and motor dysfunction; kuru is in the differential diagnosis for any adult presenting with subacute progressive ataxia.

faqs.question: What is kuru?
faqs.answer: Kuru is a fatal prion disease of the brain that was transmitted through ritual cannibalism among the Fore people of Papua New Guinea. It causes progressive cerebellar ataxia, tremors, and emotional lability before leading to death, usually within 12 months of symptom onset.

faqs.question: How was kuru transmitted?
faqs.answer: Kuru spread through endocannibalistic mortuary practices in which Fore women and children handled and consumed the brain and organs of deceased relatives. Once these practices ended after 1960, transmission stopped, though cases continued to appear for decades due to incubation periods of up to 56 years.

faqs.question: Is kuru still a threat today?
faqs.answer: No. Kuru has been effectively eliminated. No confirmed new cases have emerged since the early 2000s. Transmission ended when Fore mortuary cannibalism ceased after 1960. It poses no risk to the general public.

faqs.question: What causes kuru?
faqs.answer: Kuru is caused by prion protein — a misfolded, self-propagating form of a normal human brain protein called PrP. Unlike conventional infections, no virus, bacterium, or nucleic acid is involved. Ingested misfolded prion protein converts normal brain protein to the pathological form, progressively destroying nerve cells.

faqs.question: What are the stages of kuru?
faqs.answer: Kuru progresses through three stages. The ambulatory stage features unsteady gait, tremors, and slurred speech. The sedentary stage brings inability to walk, worsening tremors, and dementia. The terminal stage involves complete immobility, dysphagia, incontinence, and cachexia, ending in death.

faqs.question: Why is kuru called the laughing sickness?
faqs.answer: Kuru was nicknamed the laughing sickness because patients in the ambulatory and sedentary stages experienced emotional lability — episodes of uncontrollable, inappropriate laughter unrelated to actual amusement. This is a neurological symptom caused by damage to brain circuits that regulate emotion, not genuine happiness.

faqs.question: How long is the incubation period for kuru?
faqs.answer: The incubation period for kuru ranges from approximately 5 years to more than 50 years. The longest confirmed case had an incubation of roughly 56 years, in a person exposed to cannibalism before 1960 who did not develop symptoms until 2009. This extreme variability depends on the dose of prions, route of exposure, and genetic factors.

faqs.question: Is there a treatment or cure for kuru?
faqs.answer: There is no treatment that can cure or slow kuru. No drug has been shown to halt prion-induced brain damage. All care is supportive and palliative — managing symptoms, preventing complications such as aspiration pneumonia, and ensuring comfort during the terminal illness.

faqs.question: What is the prognosis for someone with kuru?
faqs.answer: Kuru is uniformly fatal. Survival after symptom onset is typically 12 months, with a range of roughly 3 months to 2 years. There are no documented cases of spontaneous recovery. Death most often results from aspiration pneumonia, sepsis from pressure ulcers, or cachexia.

faqs.question: How is kuru diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Kuru is a clinical and epidemiological diagnosis. No blood or CSF test is definitive during life. Neurological examination, MRI brain, CSF analysis (including RT-QuIC for prion protein), and PRNP gene testing provide supporting evidence. Definitive diagnosis requires brain autopsy showing spongiform changes and kuru plaques.

faqs.question: What is the difference between kuru and CJD?
faqs.answer: Both are fatal prion diseases, but kuru was spread by eating human neural tissue and begins with cerebellar ataxia and emotional lability. Sporadic CJD arises spontaneously, progresses faster (median survival 4–6 months), and typically shows characteristic EEG changes (periodic sharp waves) not seen in kuru.

faqs.question: Can kuru spread from person to person today?
faqs.answer: No. Kuru cannot spread through normal human contact — shaking hands, coughing, or sharing food does not transmit prion protein. Transmission required direct ingestion of infected neural tissue. With mortuary cannibalism ended, there is no known route of ongoing human-to-human kuru transmission.

faqs.question: What genetic factor affected who got kuru?
faqs.answer: A key genetic factor is the PRNP gene codon 129 polymorphism. People with methionine on both copies (MM homozygotes) were more susceptible to kuru and developed it at shorter incubation times. Those with one methionine and one valine copy (MV heterozygotes) had significant protection. A rare variant called G127V, found in some Fore survivors, appears to confer near-complete resistance.

faqs.question: Who discovered kuru was a prion disease?
faqs.answer: D. Carleton Gajdusek demonstrated in the 1960s that kuru could be transmitted to chimpanzees, establishing its infectious nature and winning the 1976 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine. Stanley Prusiner later showed that the infectious agent is a misfolded protein (prion), a discovery that earned him the 1997 Nobel Prize.

faqs.question: Why did kuru primarily affect women and children?
faqs.answer: Fore mortuary customs allocated brain and organ consumption primarily to women and children as a form of funerary respect, while men ate muscle tissue. Since the brain carries the highest prion concentration, women and children received far greater infectious doses, explaining the approximately 8:1 female-to-male mortality ratio at the epidemic's peak.

faqs.question: What complications does kuru cause?
faqs.answer: The main complications are aspiration pneumonia (the most common cause of death), malnutrition and cachexia, pressure ulcers, urinary tract infections, falls and fractures from ataxia, and ultimately respiratory failure. These arise from the progressive immobility, dysphagia, and incontinence that develop in the late stages.

faqs.question: Is kuru related to mad cow disease?
faqs.answer: Yes, conceptually. Both kuru and bovine spongiform encephalopathy (BSE, or mad cow disease) are prion diseases transmitted through consumption of infected neural tissue. The prion biology is analogous: BSE in cattle, like kuru in humans, is spread when animals consume feed containing prion-infected brain material. Variant CJD in humans results from eating BSE-contaminated beef.

faqs.question: How did kuru change medicine?
faqs.answer: Kuru transformed medicine by establishing that a protein alone — with no nucleic acid — can be an infectious agent, founding the entire field of prion biology. It directly influenced the recognition of sporadic CJD, the understanding of BSE and variant CJD, and the development of safety protocols for neurosurgery and blood products worldwide.

faqs.question: What should families know about caring for someone with kuru?
faqs.answer: Caring for someone with kuru is demanding because the disease is rapidly progressive and fatal. Early involvement of a palliative care team is essential. Key priorities are preventing falls, managing swallowing difficulties, maintaining skin integrity, keeping the person comfortable, and completing advance care planning while the patient can still participate in decisions.

faqs.question: Can kuru be prevented by vaccination?
faqs.answer: There is no vaccine for kuru. Prevention was achieved entirely through stopping the mortuary cannibalism practices that transmitted the disease. Research into prion immunotherapy is ongoing for other prion diseases, but no licensed vaccine exists for any human prion disorder.

metaDescription: Kuru is a fatal prion disease spread by cannibalism among Papua New Guinea's Fore people. Learn its symptoms, stages, causes, and why no treatment exists.
